---
description: "Investment strategies taxonomy — value investing (Graham, Buffett, modern), growth (GARP), momentum, quality factor, dividend strategies, factor investing (Fama-French), event-driven (M&A arb, spinoffs, distressed), macro top-down, mean reversion, when each works and fails"
---
# Investment Strategies — Taxonomy & Operational Guide

> Map of the named investment approaches an agent should be able to recognize, apply, and critique. Each strategy is a *lens* — the same company can look like a buy through one lens and a sell through another. Knowing the lens matters as much as the analysis.

## Strategy Taxonomy

| Strategy | Core thesis | Best market regime | Worst market regime |
|----------|------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Value** | Price < intrinsic value; reversion when gap closes | Rising rates, late cycle, post-bubble corrections | Long bull markets, "growth at any price" eras |
| **Growth (GARP)** | Earnings growth + reasonable price = compounding | Stable economic expansion, falling/low rates | Stagflation, multiple compression cycles |
| **Quality** | High-ROIC, low-debt, durable franchises compound | All markets, but underperforms in junk rallies | Speculative bull markets favoring leverage |
| **Momentum** | Recent winners keep winning (3-12 month horizon) | Trending markets (up or down) | Choppy / mean-reverting markets, sharp reversals |
| **Dividend** | Yield + dividend growth as core return | Low-growth, income-starved environments | Sharp risk-on rallies, rising rates |
| **Mean reversion** | Stretched moves revert | Range-bound markets, low-vol | Strong trending markets |
| **Event-driven** | Catalyst-defined return profile, market-neutral-ish | All markets (uncorrelated alpha) | Deal-arb spreads widen in stress |
| **Macro / top-down** | Asset allocation driven by economic regime | Regime transitions (rate cycles, recessions) | Stable regimes (no edge) |
| **Factor investing** | Long persistent style factors (value, momentum, quality, size, low-vol) | Long-term; factor-specific timing varies | Factor crashes (e.g., 2009 quality, 2020-21 value) |

**No strategy works in every regime.** Investors who only know one strategy will have multi-year periods of underperformance.

## Value Investing

### Graham (Deep Value, 1934)
- Buy below tangible book value, "cigar butt" approach (Graham & Dodd 1934)
- Net-net = (current assets − total liabilities) / shares; buy below 2/3 of net-net
- Margin of safety = (intrinsic value − price) / intrinsic value; require 30%+ (Graham 1949)
- Mostly extinct in liquid US markets; occasionally in micro-caps, foreign markets, post-crisis

### Buffett (Quality Value, 1965+)
- Evolved from Graham toward "wonderful business at a fair price" (Buffett 1989)
- High ROIC + durable moat + competent honest management + long runway
- Permanent capital allows long holding periods (concentrated, low turnover)
- Modern form: "owner earnings" (FCF + maintenance CapEx adjustments) vs reported earnings

### Modern Value (2010s+)
- Recognizes intangible assets (R&D, brand, network) as real but unrecorded value
- P/B is broken for asset-light businesses; uses EV/EBIT, FCF Yield, ROIC
- "Quality value" — high-ROIC value, not just statistical cheapness

### Value Traps
The single biggest risk for value investors: a low multiple that reflects deteriorating fundamentals, not a discount.
- Industries in secular decline (newspapers, traditional retail, cable TV)
- Companies losing competitive position (Kodak, Sears, Blockbuster, Intel circa 2020)
- High debt + falling earnings (leverage death spiral)
- Dividend yields >7-8% often signal pending cut, not income opportunity

**Red flag: stock has been "cheap" for 3+ years and keeps getting cheaper.** Cheap is not a catalyst.

## Growth Investing

### Pure Growth (1960s glamour era, 1990s, 2020-21)
- Buy high-growth companies regardless of valuation
- "Growth at any price" (GAAP, in a different sense)
- Works during long expansions with falling discount rates
- Catastrophic in multiple-compression environments (1973-74, 2000-02, 2022)

### GARP (Growth at a Reasonable Price)
- Peter Lynch / T. Rowe Price tradition (Lynch 1989)
- PEG ratio <1.0 historically (P/E divided by growth rate)
- Modern form: revenue growth × gross margin × payback period analysis
- Filters out the most expensive growth speculation while still owning growth

### Quality Growth
- Growth + high ROIC + low debt
- Compounders: businesses that can reinvest at high returns for 10+ years (MSFT, MA, V, ADBE, GOOG, MELI historically)
- Premium multiples justified IF growth/ROIC durability holds for 10+ years

### Common Growth-Investing Mistakes
- Extrapolating recent growth indefinitely
- Ignoring unit economics (revenue growth without contribution-margin improvement)
- Confusing TAM with capturable market
- Paying for growth that's already in the price (the "story stock" trap)
- Not recognizing that high revenue growth + persistent operating losses is often a value destroyer

## Quality Factor

### Definition
High and stable ROIC, low debt, high gross margins, consistent earnings, strong cash conversion, durable competitive position. Often combined with growth ("quality growth") or value ("quality at a price").

### Why It Works (sometimes)
Markets underprice durability. Mean reversion gets priced in too aggressively — high-ROIC businesses often stay high-ROIC longer than implied by valuations (Asness, Frazzini & Pedersen 2019).

### When It Fails
- Speculative bull markets (junk rallies — high beta + leverage win)
- Sharp risk-on rotations (early-cycle recoveries)
- Periods where quality is over-owned and expensive (2020-21)

### Common Quality Metrics
- ROIC > 15% sustained 10+ years
- Net debt / EBITDA < 2.0
- Gross margin > industry avg + stable
- FCF / Net Income > 0.9
- Earnings variability low (CV of ROE < 0.25)

## Momentum

### Cross-sectional Momentum
- Long top decile by trailing 12-month return, short bottom decile
- Skip the most recent month (avoid short-term reversal)
- Robust empirically across markets and decades (Jegadeesh & Titman 1993; Asness, Moskowitz & Pedersen 2013)

### Time-series Momentum (Trend Following)
- Long if past N-month return > 0; flat or short otherwise
- Used by CTAs / managed futures funds
- Tends to do well in crises (down trends are trends too)

### Why It Works (probably)
Underreaction to news (slow diffusion of information) + investor herding extends trends past fundamentals. Reversal eventually corrects.

### Momentum Crashes
The dark side: when leadership rotates suddenly, momentum strategies can lose 20-30% in weeks (Daniel & Moskowitz 2016). March-April 2009, January-February 2016, March 2020. Position-sizing and stops matter.

## Dividend Strategies

### Pure Yield
- Buy highest dividend yields
- **Caveat:** highest yields are often most distressed; "yield + payout ratio + dividend coverage" filter is necessary
- Common screens exclude REITs (different rules), high-yield trusts (return of capital)

### Dividend Growth
- Buy steady dividend growers (5+ year history of increases)
- "Dividend Aristocrats" (25+ years of S&P 500 dividend increases) — quality signal
- Total return = current yield + dividend growth + multiple change
- Works well in low-growth income-starved environments

### Common Mistakes
- Chasing yield into value traps
- Ignoring sector concentration (utilities, telecom, energy, REITs over-represented in high-yield)
- Tax inefficiency (dividends taxed annually; appreciation can be deferred)

## Factor Investing (Fama-French → Modern)

Academic framework: returns explained by exposure to systematic factors beyond market beta (Fama & French 1993).

### Common Factors
| Factor | What it captures | Long-term premium |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| Market (MKT-RF) | Equity risk premium | ~5-7% annually (Fama & French 1993) |
| Size (SMB) | Small caps outperform large caps long-term | ~2-3% (debated post-1980s) (Fama & French 1993) |
| Value (HML) | High book/market outperforms low book/market | ~3-4% (weak 2007-2020) (Fama & French 1993) |
| Profitability (RMW) | High operating profitability outperforms low | ~3-4% (Fama & French 2015) |
| Investment (CMA) | Conservative investment outperforms aggressive | ~2-3% (Fama & French 2015) |
| Momentum (MOM) | Recent winners outperform recent losers | ~5-7% (Carhart 1997) |
| Quality | High-quality outperforms low-quality | ~3-5% (Asness, Frazzini & Pedersen 2019). Note: "Quality" as a standalone factor is the AQR / MSCI "Quality Minus Junk" lineage and is *related to but distinct from* the Fama-French 5-factor model's Profitability (RMW) and Investment (CMA) factors. |
| Low-vol | Low-vol stocks deliver equal or better absolute returns at much lower volatility — Sharpe ratio inversely related to ex-ante volatility | The "low-vol anomaly" — a long-standing CAPM-violating empirical regularity (Frazzini & Pedersen 2014) |

### How Factor Investing Differs From Stock Picking
- Buy/sell baskets of stocks based on factor exposure (not individual conviction)
- Rebalance periodically (quarterly typical)
- Lower turnover at portfolio level than individual stock-picking
- Available via ETFs (VTV value, MTUM momentum, QUAL quality, USMV low-vol)

### Limitations
- Factor returns are noisy at short horizons (10+ years of patience required)
- Crowding can compress premia (smart beta era saw value premium compress)
- Factor crashes happen (value 2007, momentum 2009, low-vol Mar 2020)
- Factor definitions vary by provider (MSCI vs S&P vs FTSE Russell)

## Event-Driven Strategies

### Merger / Risk Arbitrage
- Buy target, short acquirer (if stock deal) after announcement
- Spread = (deal price − target price) / target price
- Wide spread = market doubts the deal closes
- Return = annualized spread × probability of close − probability of break × downside
- Catalysts: regulatory approval, shareholder vote, financing close
- Modern challenge: regulatory environment tightened 2022-2024 (FTC & DOJ 2023 Merger Guidelines; Khan-era FTC + DOJ aggressively challenged mergers), then partially reversed in 2025-2026 under new administration leadership (multiple challenges dropped or settled; merger pendulum is regime-sensitive)

### Spinoffs
- Parent splits off business unit to shareholders
- Spun entity often trades cheap initially (forced selling by index funds, lack of analyst coverage)
- Classic Greenblatt territory (Greenblatt 1997)
- Look for: insider buying post-spin, debt allocation favoring spin, clean balance sheet

### Distressed / Special Situations
- Bankruptcy reorganizations, restructurings, post-reorg equity
- High homework required, low correlation to market
- Klarman territory (Baupost) — niche, capacity-constrained (Klarman 1991)

### Other Catalysts
- Activist investor positions (often disclosed in 13D Item 4)
- Tender offers
- Liquidations
- Forced index inclusion / exclusion

## Macro / Top-Down

### Asset Allocation
- Distribute capital across asset classes (equity, fixed income, commodities, currencies, alternatives) based on regime
- Bridgewater's All Weather, risk parity approaches
- Most retail investors approximate this via target-date funds (60/40, 80/20)

### Tactical Asset Allocation (TAA)
- Shift weightings based on economic indicators
- Common signals: yield curve shape, ISM PMI, unemployment trend, credit spreads, inflation regime
- Hard to execute consistently; most active managers underperform passive 60/40

### Sector Rotation
- Cyclical sectors (industrials, materials, financials, consumer discretionary) lead in early cycle
- Defensive sectors (staples, utilities, healthcare) lead in late cycle and recessions
- Sector ETFs (XLK, XLF, XLE, XLI, XLV, XLP, XLU, XLY, XLB, XLC, XLRE) for tactical positioning

## Mean Reversion

### Statistical Pairs Trading
- Long one stock, short a correlated stock; trade the spread when it diverges
- Works in stable correlation regimes; breaks when correlations shift
- Renaissance Technologies origin story

### Short-Term Mean Reversion
- 1-5 day horizons; bet on overreaction to news
- Heavily quantitative; transaction costs critical
- Generally not for fundamentals-based investors

### Long-Term Reversion (5-10 years)
- Buy market losers of the past decade; sell market winners
- Persistent though not as strong as cross-sectional value (DeBondt & Thaler 1985)
- Behavioral root: investors over-extrapolate

## Strategy Combination

Most sophisticated investors combine strategies:
- **Quality + Value** = quality businesses temporarily out of favor
- **Quality + Growth** = compounders (the dominant institutional style 2010s-2020s)
- **Value + Momentum** = cheap stocks that are starting to work (combine signals to filter value traps)
- **Macro overlay** = strategic asset allocation, with stock-picking within asset class

Pure single-strategy approaches have decade-long drawdowns. Combination smooths returns at cost of pure-strategy alpha.

## Common Strategy-Selection Mistakes
- **Style drift** — switching strategies after underperformance (almost always wrong timing)
- **Recency bias** — adopting whichever strategy worked last 3-5 years
- **Conflating strategy with skill** — being long in a bull market isn't alpha
- **Ignoring regime** — running value through a multi-year growth rally with no risk control
- **Optimizing strategy parameters on backtest** — overfitting (see `backtesting-methodology`)

## Operational Checklist
When an agent analyzes a stock, it should be able to answer:
1. **What strategy is this thesis?** (value, growth, quality, event-driven, etc.)
2. **What regime favors this strategy?** Are we in it?
3. **What's the strategy's worst case in this name?** (value trap? growth deceleration? deal break?)
4. **Would this stock pass other strategies' screens?** (e.g., a value pick that also passes quality screens is stronger than a value-only pick)
5. **What's the typical holding period for this strategy?** (event-driven: months; value: 3-5 years; quality compounders: indefinite)
6. **What metric should be tracked over time to know the thesis is working/failing?**

## Further Reading

- **Security Analysis** — Benjamin Graham & David Dodd (1934). Foundational text for intrinsic value, balance-sheet analysis, and margin-of-safety investing.
- **The Intelligent Investor** — Benjamin Graham (1949). Practical value-investing discipline and market-psychology framework for defensive investors.
- **Berkshire Hathaway Shareholder Letters** — Warren Buffett (1977–present). Primary source for the evolution from cigar-butt value to quality-value compounding.
- **One Up on Wall Street** — Peter Lynch (1989). Accessible GARP framework focused on business understanding, growth durability, and valuation discipline.
- **Common Risk Factors in the Returns on Stocks and Bonds** — Eugene Fama & Kenneth French (1993). Introduces the three-factor model behind modern factor investing.
- **A Five-Factor Asset Pricing Model** — Eugene Fama & Kenneth French (2015). Extends factor investing with profitability and investment factors.
- **Returns to Buying Winners and Selling Losers** — Narasimhan Jegadeesh & Sheridan Titman (1993). Core empirical paper establishing cross-sectional momentum.
- **Does the Stock Market Overreact?** — Werner DeBondt & Richard Thaler (1985). Classic evidence for long-term reversal and investor overreaction.
- **Quality Minus Junk** — Clifford Asness, Andrea Frazzini & Lasse Pedersen (2019). Defines the quality factor as profitable, safe, growing, and well-managed firms.
- **You Can Be a Stock Market Genius** — Joel Greenblatt (1997). Practical guide to spinoffs and special situations where forced selling can create mispricing.
